what_it_does: This noticeboard block disputes a project brief's claim that a live element → ui:// → code:// → editable source chain is mostly in place, arguing instead that only a curated mockup-to-ui:// map and file-granular code:// cards exist and that true element-to-source-span resolution is unbuilt.
observations: It is a current state block in channel dragnet-development, linked as part_of board://item-eb5d71e7.
The title states that ui:// → code:// resolution is not wired anywhere near annotation.
It references prior items BD-J and CTX1 as the source of the overconfident claim.
It cites evidence that doc_review_server.py contains zero occurrences of ui:// or code://.
It notes that data-ui-ref machinery exists only in runtime/bridge.py and maps mockup gallery Cards to the ui:// address a mockup depicts, not live DOM elements.
It notes that code:// cards come from an archaeology dragnet keyed by file, so they identify a file rather than a specific JSX node or source span.
It lists three missing capabilities: stable ui:// tagging on every live element, an element-to-source-span resolver, and refactor survival of that resolver.
It warns that the brief's confidence could mislead a downstream agent into editing the wrong place or an entire file.
standouts: It is a direct rebuttal of a done-in-principle claim backed by named file evidence.
It clearly distinguishes a hand-maintained mockup-gallery mapping from live DOM element-to-source resolution.
It reframes the gap as a hard source-mapping / build-time instrumentation problem rather than a simple wiring task.
conventions: Follows the ledger item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses board:// URI addressing and a part_of link.
Uses Markdown body with a heading, bold emphasis, inline code formatting, and a bulleted list.
Cites external artifacts and prior item IDs by name without embedding them as metadata links.
concerns: The claims about other files are not verifiable from this file alone.
The tone is strongly argumentative, using words like zero, not, and will mislead.
It does not provide line numbers, commit references, or version identifiers for the cited evidence.
notes: A skeptical audit note arguing that live element-to-source editing is still an unbuilt capability, not a near-ready feature.
questions: What exact claims do BD-J and CTX1 make, and where are they located?
Which version or commit of doc_review_server.py and runtime/bridge.py was inspected?
Are there any other consumers of data-ui-ref beyond the mockup gallery map?
What does annotation refer to in the title?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to correct an overconfident brief about ui://→code:// wiring, and the file does exactly that by presenting counter-evidence and missing requirements, so purpose matches actual.
apparent_themes: Granularity matters: file-level identity is not element-level identity.
Live runtime instrumentation is different from curated mockup mapping.
Avoiding misleading downstream agents about edit readiness.
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to stop a project plan from underestimating the work required to connect rendered UI elements directly to precise editable source spans and their dependencies.
novelty: It reframes what sounds like a resolver wiring gap as a fundamental source-mapping and build-time instrumentation problem, separating curated mockup addresses from live DOM element addresses.
contribution: It contributes a grounded counter-argument and a precise decomposition of what element-to-source editing actually requires, which a fused system should keep to avoid planning on false assumptions.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item arguing that the project brief's claim of a working element→ui://→code://→editable-source chain is unsupported, because only a hand-maintained mockup-to-ui:// map and file-granular code:// cards exist, leaving live element-to-source-span resolution unbuilt.
intention_for_embedding: To warn that downstream planning should treat direct element-to-source editing as a separate, hard instrumentation problem rather than an already-wired capability.
